episode_title: Jeff Jarvis (Part One): Should AI have the 'right to read' news like humans do?

codes.description: The provocative framing that AI companies should have the same right to read and learn from published content as human journalists do
excerpts: Does the machine have the same right to read and learn and use information as a journalist? Because every journalist on Earth reads other journalists' work and gets information from sources and does not pay them, learns from it and uses it and repurposes it in their journalism.

does the company making the machine or the user of the machine or the program, whether that's Google or OpenAI, have the same rights as WCBS radio or clear channel radio to rip and read stories out of the newspaper
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The critical legal and ethical distinction between AI training on content (learning language patterns) and AI querying/outputting specific content from publications
excerpts: content that's used to just train the program how to speak and how to understand what we're saying... The query or output is different insofar as I ask it a question, does it come back to me and give me content that is in fact from a publication
I think the training is fair use because it is transformative
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The historical fact that newspapers were excluded from US copyright until 1909, undermining claims that news has always been copyright-protected
excerpts: Newspapers were not included in copyright in in this original formulation in 1710 in England, was not included in the U. S. copyright at first. Only maps, books, and charts were
Copyright was not included until 1909 for newspapers
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The historical practice of newspapers employing 'scissors editors' who literally cut content from other papers and reprinted it for free, establishing a precedent for content sharing
excerpts: Newspapers employed people called scissors editors, their actual job title, where they would cut things out of others' newspapers and then get them typeset and print them, and they they wanted credit. But it was for free.
the US Postal Act of seventeen ninety two enabled newspapers to send each other copies of their papers for free. And this was an intentional way to set up a network for news
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The conflict created when news organizations lobby the politicians they claim to be independent of, creating indebtedness that undermines editorial independence
excerpts: I'm upset about my fellow journalists and news organizations. Number one, that they are lobbying the politicians they should be independent of. And they shouldn't be in a position to seek favors and be indebted to politicians.
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: News organizations repeating their historical pattern of trying to suppress new technologies (radio, TV, internet) through restrictive agreements and legislation
excerpts: when radio came along Newspapers tried to keep them out of the news business. They made them sign an agreement that said that they would do no news or that they would do only news only twice a day and that news would have to come from the wire services
They tried to keep journalists out of congressional press galleries. That was the reception of newspapers to radio, and to T V, and now to the Internet, and then to AI
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: Jarvis dismissing the New York Times' demonstration of GPT reproducing articles verbatim as a contrived demonstration rather than evidence of normal model behavior
excerpts: They went through a lot of hoops to get the machine to repeat something. They were they gave them lots of paragraphs of an article, and kind of the only words left for it to predict were those that were left
that was a parlor trick on a parlor trick. And I don't think it's the real issue here
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: Proposing a technology-first solution where news organizations create an API with keys and contracts, allowing AI companies to query and serve news under negotiated terms
excerpts: we in the news industry should be creating an API to news. It's the way technology people think. Why wouldn't we create an API with keys that we can give out under contracts with limitations for news
the model would be free to come back to a query and say Well yes, in answer to your question, I'll give you the background, but here's what the New York Times says
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The argument that AI's biggest threat is making the production of content trivial, while the true value of journalism lies in reporting, trust, authority, and service
excerpts: the biggest threat that AI brings to our business is that it commodifies content. Now comes a machine that can make no end of content. And making content ain't so special. Writing isn't so special. Reporting is. Trust is, authority is, service is. That's where the value of journalism should be resident
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The argument that journalism has become overly fixated on 'content' as its primary value proposition, when the real value should reside in relationships, trust, and service
excerpts: I think that we in journalism have gotten too obsessive about this idea of content. And we think that our value is entirely resonant in this thing we make called content
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: Jarvis objecting to the term 'hallucination' for LLM factual errors, noting models don't lie but simply have no concept of truth
excerpts: what I hate when they call it hallucination, but the fact that it has no sense of fact. It doesn't lie, it doesn't know what truth is
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The practical limitation that LLMs are only trained up to a certain date and lack real-time information, creating an opportunity for news organizations
excerpts: The second is currency. the models only go up to a certain date

I see a value in news and not just news in Wikipedia and and other sources. in saying we're gonna make it easy for you that when the model hits something where it knows uh it's been trained to know what it doesn't know
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The strategic recommendation that news organizations should negotiate constructively with AI companies rather than seeking courtroom victories or legislative handouts
excerpts: Rather than fighting at this point, as we are out of our out of our field. We should, I think, be looking for ways to make it possible for AI companies to query And serve information from us and licensed for that purpose
I think that there are opportunities for us to just think more positively about this rather than going to Congress and courts. And thinking that if we win, we'll get a bucket of money
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The concern that OpenAI's early licensing deals with Springer and AP established commercial norms before legal and ethical principles were properly debated
excerpts: I do think that OpenAI probably made a tactical error in negotiating with Springer and the Associated Press, because it sets a precedent before the principles are set. It's trying to say, well, don't sue us. Here it is.
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: Defending Common Crawl's archiving of the open web as entirely legitimate, and criticizing the NYT for demanding their free content be removed from it
excerpts: Common Crawl, which is an open and free protocol that goes and archives the textual web, it's been mainly for researchers... That's the open and free web. And that should be just their right to do

The New York Times has demanded of Common Crawl that they take down their content, even that archival of content that was free. Well, I think that's a moral issue of the New York Times trying to deprecate the value of our web
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: Acknowledging that while fair use may cover training, the acquisition of training data like Books3 raises ethical questions since creators didn't purchase every book included
excerpts: Books 3 is obviously different, because the creators of that database, which again was done for scholarship, was incredibly valuable for scholarship. But once large language models came along, uh had different set of value, and they didn't buy every copy of those books
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: Jarvis's personal admission that he would feel miffed if his books weren't included in AI training data, revealing the emotional/ego dimension of the copyright debate
excerpts: I, of course, egotistically went and looked up my first books to see if they were there. And they were, and if they hadn't been, I would have been really miffed and hurt. I would have felt really unimportant because I don't write just to make money, I write to get ideas out there
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: Reframing news stories as just another form of data visualization alongside charts and infographics, challenging the idea that news content has unique ownership claims
excerpts: I argue that that news stories are just another form of data visualization. You could make a chart, you could make a box, you could make a story
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The critical insight that LLM training data overwhelmingly reflects those who had the privilege to publish, reproducing dominant biases, cliches, and worldviews
excerpts: the models are reflecting back to us the publishing of the privileged. To your point about the rest of the world and even within the world, those who didn't publish, those who didn't have things there
All it's playing back to us is our cliches and our biases and our misapprehensions about the world as it is
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The degradation that occurs when AI models train on AI-generated content, producing progressively worse outputs in a feedback loop
excerpts: when models spew out content and train on that content, we end up in the end with a gray goo
the models are ingesting AI content, and that kind of makes them go crazy a bit. and spit out things and the model doesn't work as well
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis / Nikita Roy

codes.description: The massive opportunity to digitize content from underrepresented regions, particularly Africa, as a way to address training data gaps before resorting to synthetic data
excerpts: Just think about all of the countries that are still not as digitized. One of the things we had spoken about was like in the African continent. A lot of the governments over there, everything that is out there it's and is digital is actually written from a Western perspective
what if we go and invest heavily on making Transforming things from print to digital
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: The possibility that LLMs could extend literacy by enabling people intimidated by writing to speak their stories and have machines help articulate them
excerpts: LLMs might extend literacy to the extent that they enable people who are intimidated by writing or illustrating to write or illustrate their own stories
being able to speak to the machine, being able to hear back, being able to have the machine give you back the things that you want to say, I think is quite intriguing
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The critique that AI-mediated writing assistance forces marginalized communities to conform to the 'white hegemony of language' embedded in training data
excerpts: Two of my students, one who has a different serving people who are imprisoned, another First Nations in Canada, said, whoa, Jarvis, then you're making everybody code switch. to the white hegemony of language, because it's gonna mimic that which came from those who had the privilege to publish
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis (relaying students' critique)

codes.description: The characterization of LLMs as a fascinating but warped, cracked, and inadequate mirror of society that we must play with while recognizing its limitations
excerpts: It's a cracked mirror, it's a warped mirror, it's an inadequate mirror. It's a fascinating mirror, and it's a mirror we can play with and do things with, but we must recognize its limitations
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: Questioning whether a news-specific LLM would be valuable given that news data itself is not representative of the world's diversity
excerpts: if you're just gonna be training on news data, how much of the news so far, let's say, has really been representative of the world?
we have now all of these conversations over about DEI and diversity and having making sure that we're including more diverse perspectives
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: The challenge to the 'rebuilding trust' narrative: many communities never trusted news in the first place
excerpts: when I used the phrase rebuilding trust in news. And I think it was one of my students who said, whoa, there's a lot of people who never trusted news. And I think that's absolutely true
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The analytical opportunity to use LLM training data as a concordance to discover and expose the cliches, biases, and blind spots in published knowledge
excerpts: if I had this concordance of all the available text in the world, rather than having it spit out more of the same, what interests me is to be able to query that data to find our cliches and our biases and our misapprehensions and our connections
it could also tell us what's missing
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The historical argument that copyright was originally designed to create a tradable marketplace for publishers, not to protect individual authors as commonly believed
excerpts: at the very beginning in 1710, it was booksellers and publishers who demanded a system that would create a marketplace for creativity as a tradable asset
The author was protected in the sense that they could sell it in the first place, but then it was no longer theirs
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The concept of tracking a chain of creative contributions rather than attributing creation to a single romantic author figure
excerpts: I came up with this concept that I call credit rate. that argues that there's a string of contributions to creativity. If somebody you see somebody out there who does something interesting and you write a poem about it, then I come along and I write song lyrics about it. Somebody else comes along and writes music for that.
Every one of those actors along that way deserves some credit for contributing to the creative product in the end
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The unresolved question of where liability should fall in the AI stack: at the model level (absurd, like suing Gutenberg), the application level, or the user level
excerpts: If it lies at the model level then there's this absurd thing where it's like trying to go back and sue Gutenberg for everything that's ever been printed

If Microsoft puts up ChatGPT on Bing and doesn't make clear that it doesn't do facts, and you're the poor lawyer who comes along and asks it for case citations... Then maybe Microsoft should be liable for misleading
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: The warning against writing AI laws based on current capabilities, which would miss the trajectory of technological development
excerpts: if we would be the risk of regulating and creating laws around this right now Is that we don't know how they are going to be changing the world basically. And if if we go down and write laws for what we expected to do today, we would completely miss this path of technological development
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy (citing Rebecca Tushnet)

codes.description: The argument that concerns about AI-driven job displacement should be addressed through labor and unfair competition laws, not copyright
excerpts: if you care about the potential for lost jobs and that generative AI models would bring about, we need to look to labor laws and unfair competition laws. and not copyright. Copyright's not gonna help you with that
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy (citing Rebecca Tushnet)

codes.description: Canada's C-18 legislation as a warning: when forced to pay for news, Meta simply dropped news entirely, leaving publishers worse off
excerpts: in C18 in Canada with social media. the news organizations tried to get money out of Google and Facebook. Facebook just dropped news entirely
now I think we're going to see the same thing happen with AI
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.code: Fair use as right to hire lawyer
codes.description: The inherent ambiguity of fair use doctrine, which provides no clear-cut answers and will require costly litigation to resolve in the AI context
excerpts: Larry Lessig of Harvard famously said that fair use is the right to hire a lawyer. It's not set in stone. We're gonna have arguments about this in court.
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.code: Shrinking fair use vs expanding it
codes.description: The accusation from both sides that the other is destroying copyright: publishers say Jarvis expands fair use to make copyright meaningless, he says they shrink it to the same effect
excerpts: they're thus, with the legislators, with the Senate, trying to shrink fair use to the extent that copyright won't exist. Now I was accused later on in the hearing of expanding fair use so much that copyright wouldn't exist
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

codes.description: Sam Altman's assertion at Davos that future models will learn from smaller amounts of higher quality data, analogous to learning high school biology from one textbook
excerpts: Sam Altman actually just this week at Davos in the World Economic Forum spoke about how we actually don't need to train on the New York Times data and was talking about how these the change that he's expecting in these models is that they would be able to take smaller amounts of higher quality data
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy (reporting Sam Altman)

codes.description: The historical insight that the concept of authorship emerged not from creativity but from the need to identify who could be held liable for published words
excerpts: our sense of authorship... didn't really come until the point at which authors were held liable for their words. In the early days of print It was the printer who would get beheaded or behanded for what they printed
codes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

episode_summary: This episode mounts a systematic historical and legal argument that the news industry's aggressive copyright claims against AI companies are not only strategically self-defeating but also historically inconsistent with journalism's own traditions of information sharing. Jarvis's 'scissors editors' example and the 1792 Postal Act reveal that free content circulation was foundational to American journalism, directly contradicting the NYT's claim of longstanding copyright protection. The episode's most analytically important contribution is the training-vs-query distinction: conceding that training is fair use while arguing for licensable query mechanisms (like a news API) offers a practical middle ground the industry is largely ignoring in favor of litigation. The discussion also surfaces a deeper existential challenge: AI commodifies the production of content, which forces journalism to relocate its value proposition from the artifact (the article) to the relationships and processes (reporting, trust, authority, service) that produce it. This reframing, if taken seriously, would require a fundamental rethinking of news business models.
surprises: Jeff Jarvis admits he would be 'miffed and hurt' if his books were NOT included in AI training data, revealing an ego dimension that cuts against the copyright protection narrative
Newspapers were excluded from US copyright until 1909 and the 1792 Postal Act intentionally enabled free content sharing between newspapers, directly contradicting the NYT's historical claims
Jarvis's students corrected his 'rebuilding trust' framing by pointing out many communities never trusted news in the first place
The suggestion to use LLMs not for output generation but as analytical tools to discover biases, cliches, and gaps in our published knowledge inverts the typical use case
The argument that news stories are 'just another form of data visualization' alongside charts and infographics, challenging the special status of journalistic content
tensions: Jarvis argues training is fair use but acknowledges Books3 raised legitimate acquisition ethics issues, creating an inconsistency in his own framework
LLMs could extend literacy to marginalized communities but simultaneously force them to code-switch to dominant language norms embedded in training data
The training-vs-query distinction offers a pragmatic middle ground, but OpenAI's premature licensing deals with Springer and AP have already muddied the principled debate
Jarvis calls for negotiation over litigation, but the industry's loss-aversion PTSD (from giving content away for free to internet platforms) makes cooperative approaches politically impossible
The 'content is commodified' argument implies journalism must find new value propositions, but most publishers are still investing heavily in content production as their core business
The C-18 example shows platforms will simply drop news if forced to pay, but doing nothing allows unrestricted use without compensation, creating a lose-lose for publishers
